content.text: Every unnecessary token in a prompt is money spent without return. Prompt efficiency — reducing input token count without degrading output quality — is the most deterministic cost lever available because it applies to every request, on every model tier. A 50% prompt reduction cuts input costs by 50% with no trade-off when the removed content was not contributing to the response.

attrs.alt: Bloated vs optimized prompt token breakdown showing 74% cost reduction

content.text: Measure First
Before optimizing, instrument your prompts so you know exactly what you're paying for. The
count_tokens
endpoint lets you measure any prompt without spending real inference cost:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def audit_prompt(system: str, messages: list, tools: list = None) -> dict:
    """Break down token usage by component before spending on a real request."""
    kwargs = {
        "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
        "system": system,
        "messages": messages,
    }
    if tools:
        kwargs["tools"] = tools

    total = client.messages.count_tokens(**kwargs).input_tokens

    # Measure components individually
    system_only = client.messages.count_tokens(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "x"}],
    ).input_tokens - 10  # subtract minimal message overhead

    last_msg_tokens = client.messages.count_tokens(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        system="x",
        messages=messages[-1:],
    ).input_tokens - 5

    tool_overhead = 0
    if tools:
        no_tools = client.messages.count_tokens(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
            system=system,
            messages=messages,
        ).input_tokens
        with_tools = total
        tool_overhead = with_tools - no_tools

    return {
        "total":          total,
        "system_approx":  system_only,
        "latest_msg":     last_msg_tokens,
        "tool_overhead":  tool_overhead,
        "history_approx": total - system_only - last_msg_tokens - tool_overhead,
    }
Trimming System Prompts
System prompts are paid on every request, making them the highest ROI target for reduction. Common patterns that add tokens without adding value:
Restating the same instruction multiple times
: 'Always be helpful. Remember to be helpful. Make sure you help the user.' — says the same thing three times. Say it once.
Preamble that explains context instead of defining behavior

: 'You are an AI assistant built by Acme Corp for customer support purposes to help users with their accounts...' — 40 tokens of metadata Claude does not need to do its job. Write 'Handle customer account inquiries. Escalate billing disputes to human agents.'
Listing non-behaviors
: 'Do not write malware. Do not produce harmful content. Do not...' — Claude's defaults already include these constraints. Listing them wastes tokens without changing behavior.
Verbose format descriptions
: 'Please structure your response with a clear introduction, followed by the main content organized into logical sections, and conclude with a summary...' → 'Format: intro, sections, summary.'

# Before: 320 tokens
SYSTEM_VERBOSE = """
You are an AI assistant designed to help users with questions about our
software product. You should always be polite and professional in your
responses. When users ask questions, make sure to provide accurate and
helpful information. Always remember to be concise and clear. You should
not discuss topics unrelated to our software. If users ask about
competitor products, politely decline to compare. Make sure your
responses are formatted clearly and easy to read. Always greet the user
when they first start a conversation.
"""

# After: 52 tokens
SYSTEM_EFFICIENT = """
Answer questions about our software product only. Be concise.
Decline competitor comparisons politely.
"""
Controlling Conversation History Growth

In multi-turn conversations, history grows linearly and compounds across requests. A 20-turn chat where each turn averages 300 tokens accumulates 6,000 tokens of history — paying for the entire past conversation on every new turn.
Fixed Window Truncation
The simplest approach: keep only the last N turns. Works when each turn is self-contained:

def truncate_history(history: list[dict], max_turns: int = 10) -> list[dict]:
    """Keep only the most recent max_turns complete exchanges."""
    # Each 'turn' = one user + one assistant message = 2 entries
    keep = max_turns * 2
    if len(history) <= keep:
        return history
    return history[-keep:]
Token Budget Truncation
More precise: trim to stay under a token ceiling, always keeping the most recent turns:

def budget_truncate_history(
    history: list[dict],
    system: str,
    new_message: str,
    token_budget: int = 3000,
    model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-6",
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Remove oldest messages until the full prompt fits within token_budget.
    Always keeps at least the most recent user-assistant exchange.
    """
    # Measure overhead from system + new message
    overhead = client.messages.count_tokens(
        model=model,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": new_message}],
    ).input_tokens

    available = token_budget - overhead
    trimmed = list(history)

    while len(trimmed) > 2:  # keep at minimum 1 exchange
        full = client.messages.count_tokens(
            model=model,
            system=system,
            messages=trimmed + [{"role": "user", "content": new_message}],
        ).input_tokens
        if full - overhead <= available:
            break
        trimmed = trimmed[2:]  # drop oldest exchange

    return trimmed
Summarization Compression
When history must be retained but token count must shrink, compress old turns into a summary:
Efficient Tool Definitions
Tool schemas are injected on every request that includes them, regardless of whether the tool is called. An agent with 50 tools pays for all 50 schemas whether it uses 1 or all of them.
Selective Tool Loading

# Full tool catalog — loaded from config, not always sent
ALL_TOOLS = load_tools_from_config()

def get_relevant_tools(user_intent: str, all_tools: list) -> list:
    """Return only tools likely needed for this intent."""
    # Option A: rule-based routing
    if "weather" in user_intent.lower():
        return [t for t in all_tools if t["name"] in ("get_weather", "get_forecast")]
    if "calendar" in user_intent.lower():
        return [t for t in all_tools if t["name"].startswith("calendar_")]
    # Default: send the 5 most common tools
    return all_tools[:5]

# Before: 50 tools × ~150 tokens each = 7,500 token overhead
# After: 3 tools × ~150 tokens each = 450 token overhead
# 94% reduction in tool overhead
Compact Tool Descriptions
Tool descriptions are natural language and expand easily. Audit descriptions for verbosity:
Verbose (42 tokens)
Compact (12 tokens)
Searches the product database to find products matching the given search criteria. Returns a list of matching products with their details.
Search products by query. Returns list of matches.
Gets the current weather conditions and forecast for a specified location using real-time weather data.
Get weather for a city. Returns current conditions.
Trim descriptions to the minimum that tells Claude what the tool does and when to use it. Unnecessary prose does not improve Claude's tool selection accuracy.
Output Efficiency
Since output tokens cost 5× more than input, over-verbose responses compound cost. Control output length through both
max_tokens
and prompt instructions:

# Bad: open-ended max_tokens invites verbosity
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=4096,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is Python?"}],
)

# Better: tight max_tokens + explicit instruction
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=150,
    system="Answer concisely. 1-3 sentences max unless the task requires more.",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is Python?"}],
)
Instructions that reliably reduce output length:
Answer in N sentences.
Return only the JSON object, no explanation.
List format only. No prose.
One word answer.
Be brief.
Measuring Prompt Efficiency Over Time
Track average input tokens per request per endpoint. An upward trend signals that history or system prompt bloat is accumulating:
Prompt Efficiency Checklist
Measure before optimizing
: Use
count_tokens()
to establish a baseline per component.
Audit system prompt
: Remove redundancy, metadata, and non-behavior descriptions. Target < 500 tokens for most applications.
Truncate history
: Implement either fixed-window or token-budget truncation. Do not send unbounded history.
Selective tools
: Send only tools relevant to the current task. Measure tool overhead per endpoint.
Trim tool descriptions
: One sentence per tool. Test that trimmed descriptions do not degrade tool selection accuracy.
Set tight max_tokens
: Profile p99 output length and set ceiling accordingly.
Add brevity instructions
: Explicit length constraints in the system prompt reliably reduce output tokens.
Monitor over time
: Track average input tokens per endpoint weekly. Investigate upward trends.
Summary

Prompt efficiency is the most deterministic cost control available — every token you remove saves money on every request, forever, on every model tier. The main levers are system prompt compression (remove redundancy and non-behaviors), history truncation (fixed window or token budget), selective tool loading (send only needed tools), and output length control (tight
max_tokens
plus brevity instructions). Instrument with
count_tokens()
before and after to verify reductions, and monitor average input tokens per endpoint to catch drift.
